choroidal_neovascularization: The image shows retinal thickening and hyporeflective cystic areas, resembling diabetic macular edema.
Hyperreflective lesions with subretinal or intraretinal fluid are present, features more typical of choroidal neovascularization.
Small, dome-shaped elevations of the RPE layer are seen, mimicking drusen.
Pigment epithelial detachment and disruption of normal retinal layers are present, which may be confused with neovascularization.
Diffuse or focal swelling of the central retina is present, features more typical of diabetic macular edema.
diabetic_macular_edema: The image shows retinal thickening and hyporeflective cystic areas, resembling diabetic macular edema rather than neovascularization.
There are small, dome-shaped elevations of the RPE layer without significant fluid, features more typical of drusen.
The scan displays well-defined retinal layers with no signs of abnormal vessels or fluid, mimicking a normal OCT scan.
Diffuse or focal swelling of the central retina is present, which may be confused with diabetic macular edema.
Multiple discrete mounds under the retina are seen, resembling drusen rather than neovascularization.
drusen: The image shows retinal thickening and hyporeflective cystic areas, resembling diabetic macular edema.
Hyperreflective lesions with subretinal or intraretinal fluid are present, features more typical of choroidal neovascularization.
The scan displays well-defined retinal layers with no abnormal deposits, mimicking a normal OCT scan.
Diffuse swelling of the central retina and intraretinal fluid are seen, which may be confused with diabetic macular edema.
Pigment epithelial detachment and disruption of normal retinal layers are present, features more typical of neovascularization.
normal_OCT_scan: The image shows hyperreflective lesions with subretinal fluid, features more typical of choroidal neovascularization.
There are small, dome-shaped elevations of the RPE layer without cystoid changes, resembling drusen.
The scan displays well-defined retinal layers and no evidence of fluid or thickening, mimicking a normal OCT scan.
Pigment epithelial detachment and disruption of normal retinal layers are present, which may be confused with neovascularization.
Multiple discrete mounds under the retina are seen, features more typical of drusen.
